text: The surgeon incorporates the omentum from the transverse colon into the gallbladder area, with visible inflammatory flakes appearing in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the adhesions between the gallbladder and the omentum of the transverse colon. The gallbladder appears thick-walled and distended, making it difficult to manipulate.

The surgeon uses a needle to aspirate pus and infected bile from the distended gallbladder. A tooth clasper is then applied at the level of the gallbladder fundus to provide frontal traction, aiding in the surgical procedure.

The surgeon applies traction towards the patient's right shoulder while dissecting small adhesions around the inflamed gallbladder. Due to inflammation, there is noticeable oozing, which is managed using gauze.

The surgeon uses a gauze with a blunt tip to apply pressure, opening up tissue planes without damaging the gallbladder or surrounding structures. Dissection continues at the level of the anterior peritoneum, revealing fibrotic inflammation in the region.

The surgeon employs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to identify anatomical planes and landmarks, focusing on the small adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding tissues.

The surgeon gradually takes down the adhesions using sharp dissection with a low watt electrocautery set at around twenty watts. Care is taken to ensure the gallbladder is kept away from the area where energy is being applied.
The surgeon adjusts the area of traction to safely dissect and divide small fibrotic adhesions around the gallbladder, using careful technique to manage the inflamed tissue.

The surgeon encounters thick and fibrotic adhesions that require sharp dissection with monopolar energy, as blunt dissection is insufficient. A gauze is used to maintain the surgical field, and suction is applied to the gauze to prevent the collapse of pneumoperitoneum.

The surgeon applies traction on the tissues while simultaneously pushing the gallbladder away from the intended resection area, minimizing trauma to surrounding structures and reducing oozing and bleeding.

The surgeon reveals more anatomical structures as pus is observed emanating from the area previously adherent to the gallbladder. Care is taken to ensure that the duodenum, located nearby, is not involved, avoiding any cystic communication or perforation.

The surgeon uses a traumatic grasper to hold the gallbladder near a small iatrogenic perforation site, causing infected bile to leak out. The bile is visibly dark in color and thick in consistency. The surgeon then proceeds with further dissection below the perforation site.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and demonstrates the Rouviere's sulcus, ensuring dissection remains above this landmark. The common bile duct (CBD) is visible, with the cystic duct appearing short and wide.
The surgeon performs a subtotal cholecystectomy, carefully leaving approximately one centimeter of tissue near the common bile duct (CBD) to ensure safety and avoid damage to surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses energy to divide tissue at the lower portion of the gallbladder near the neck and cystic duct region, leaving a one-centimeter margin for safety. The remnant tissue is sutured to obliterate the cystic duct opening.

The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring the incision remains parallel to the initial site to avoid going deeper and causing damage to the liver or major vessels. The posterior dissection lifts the gallbladder, revealing the anatomical level of cutting.

The interior of the gallbladder is visibly gangrenous with sloughing tissue. The surgeon emphasizes the importance of erring on the side of caution by dividing closer to the gallbladder rather than near the common bile duct (CBD) to avoid complications.

The surgeon completes the division of the final attachments and adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding tissues. At the posterior level of the transaction site, a vessel, likely the cystic artery or its branch, is identified and secured with a titanium clip. Scissors are used to sharply divide the distal portion of the vessel, resulting in minimal back bleeding.

The surgeon completes the ligation and proceeds to remove the remaining adhesions between the gallbladder and the liver bed. A suction cannula is used to identify the correct anatomical plane, ensuring the surgical field is clear of potential hazards.

The surgeon observes the gangrenous transformation of the gallbladder wall, with only slough tissue remaining. Due to proximity to major vessels in the porta hepatis area, a small portion of the posterior wall is left intact. The surgeon then proceeds to remove the entire gallbladder in total by moving superiorly.

The surgeon leaves a small portion of the posterior gallbladder wall intact, using alternating energy and suction to coagulate the surface and maintain a dry surgical field. The energy device is applied meticulously to avoid penetrating too deeply into the liver bed.

The surgeon uses energy intermittently to dissect the gallbladder wall, ensuring not to leave excess tissue or penetrate deeply into the liver parenchyma. The dissection is performed carefully in the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon identifies the area inferior to the dissection where a small portion of the posterior wall of the gallbladder was left intact. Above the dissection area, the liver is visible, and the gallbladder has been completely removed. The remaining tissue is being divided to proceed with the surgery.

The surgeon performs sharp and blunt dissection to separate the final attachments of the gallbladder, using traction to identify the correct anatomical plane and achieve hemostasis despite generalized oozing due to inflammation.

The surgeon continues to identify dissection planes using energy devices, carefully pushing away gangrenous tissue remnants to ensure complete removal of the gallbladder. If excessive adhesion to the liver bed prevents total removal, the posterior wall may be left intact while excising the rest of the gallbladder.

The surgeon carefully dissects the last adhesions attaching the gallbladder to the liver bed. A small portion remains, while the rest of the area appears edematous and discolored. The gallbladder is used for traction before dividing the final attachment.

The surgeon uses a 2-0 Y-Curl needle to take bites on both edges of the divided tissue, aiming to obliterate the space and close the opening of the cystic duct. The needle's curvature is carefully maneuvered to avoid injury to surrounding structures, and a surgeon's knot is applied to secure the closure.

The surgeon tightens the knot securely, adding additional throws to ensure stability, similar to an open procedure. A second bite is taken with careful needle direction, positioned approximately 1.5 centimeters from the confluence of the cystic duct and the common bile duct. The proximity to the common bile duct is now clearly visible, despite the initial appearance of abundant tissue.

The surgeon takes the third and final suture bite on the tissue near the cystic duct, ensuring a secure hold. The knot is tightened to seal off the area effectively, and the loop along with other sutures are cut to complete the closure.

The surgeon uses a suture for traction to identify the area and secure hemostasis. The common bile duct (CBD) is visible, with the confluence of the cystic duct just above it. The surgeon then shifts focus back to the gallbladder, taking down the final attachments to complete the cholecystectomy, with visible changes in traction positions.

The surgeon carefully dissects the final flimsy attachments of the gallbladder from the liver bed, proceeding slowly to avoid inadvertent injury and bleeding. The dissection is performed in the correct anatomical planes to minimize difficulty.
The surgeon carefully dissects the final portion of the attachment between the gallbladder and the liver bed, completing the cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon completes the division of the gallbladder from the liver bed and turns attention to securing hemostasis. The surgical field shows minimal bleeding, and the gallbladder specimens are placed into an endobag for retrieval.

The surgeon places the gallbladder into an indigenously made endobag, ensuring the fundus is positioned towards the distal portion and the neck near the opening for easy retrieval. Sutures are used to close the bag, sealing it completely for removal. An abdominal drain is placed subhepatically.
